1.name: User Login & Registration
1.keywords: login register signup authentication user account password
1.why_frontend: React login form with real-time validation. JWT stored in httpOnly cookie — NOT localStorage (XSS vulnerable).
1.why_backend: Spring Security handles auth out of the box. BCrypt hashes passwords — never store plain text.
1.why_database: MySQL stores user credentials permanently. Redis stores blacklisted JWT tokens — when user logs out token is blacklisted instantly.
1.why_pattern: Stateless Auth means server stores nothing. JWT contains all user info. Scales to millions of users without session storage.
1.security: BCrypt cost factor 12, JWT expiry 15 min, Refresh token 7 days, 5 failed attempts = account lock, Rate limit login endpoint
1.rbi_rules: RBI mandates strong authentication for financial apps. Password minimum 8 chars with special characters. Session timeout 15 minutes.
1.skills: React, Java, Spring Security, JWT, BCrypt, MySQL, Redis
1.how_it_works: User enters email + password → React sends to Spring Boot → BCrypt verifies hash → JWT generated → Stored in httpOnly cookie → Every API request sends JWT in header → Spring Security validates on every request
1.real_world: Paytm, PhonePe, GPay all use this exact JWT + BCrypt pattern

2.name: Two Factor Authentication (OTP)
2.keywords: otp two factor 2fa mobile verification sms secure
2.protocols: HTTPS, JWT, TOTP RFC 6238, SMS via Twilio
2.why_frontend: React OTP input with 6 separate boxes. Auto submits when all 6 entered. Countdown timer shows OTP validity.
2.why_backend: Spring Boot generates 6 digit OTP. Stores in Redis with 300 second TTL. Redis auto-deletes after expiry — no manual cleanup.
2.why_database: Redis TTL feature perfect for OTP. SET otp:userId 123456 EX 300 — OTP auto expires in 5 minutes.
2.why_pattern: Two factors: something you know (password) + something you have (phone). Even stolen password cannot login without OTP.
2.security: OTP valid 5 minutes only, One time use, Max 3 attempts then lockout, OTP hashed in Redis, TRAI DLT registration for SMS
2.rbi_rules: RBI mandates OTP for transactions above Rs 5000. OTP mandatory for new payee addition. Pre-registration on TRAI DLT for SMS.
2.skills: React, Java, Spring Boot, Redis, Twilio or MSG91
2.how_it_works: Password verified → Spring Boot generates OTP → Hashed OTP stored Redis with 5 min TTL → SMS sent via Twilio → User enters OTP → Spring Boot hashes entered OTP → Compares with Redis → Match: JWT issued → No match: attempt count incremented
flow_steps: OTP hashed and stored in Redis (TTL 300s)
SMS sent via Twilio to registered mobile
User enters OTP in React input
Match: delete from Redis, issue JWT
2.real_world: All Indian banking apps use this per RBI mandate. Paytm, HDFC, ICICI all verified.

3.name: JWT Token Management
3.keywords: jwt token refresh expire session management security
3.protocols: HTTPS, JWT RS256, Refresh Token Rotation
3.why_frontend: Axios interceptor automatically refreshes expired JWT. User never gets logged out unexpectedly. Transparent token refresh in background.
3.why_backend: Spring Boot validates JWT on every request using filter chain. If expired: check refresh token in Redis. If valid: issue new JWT.
3.why_database: Redis stores refresh tokens with 7 day TTL. On logout: delete refresh token from Redis — cannot get new JWT anymore.
3.why_pattern: Short JWT (15 min) limits damage if token stolen. Refresh token rotation means old refresh tokens cannot be reused.
3.security: JWT expiry 15 minutes, Refresh token 7 days, Token rotation on every refresh, Blacklist on logout, RS256 asymmetric signing
3.rbi_rules: Session timeout mandatory for financial apps. RBI requires re-authentication for sensitive operations.
3.skills: React, Axios, Java, Spring Boot, JWT library, Redis
3.how_it_works: React makes API call → JWT expired → Axios interceptor catches 401 → Automatically calls /auth/refresh with refresh token → Spring Boot validates refresh token from Redis → Issues new JWT + new refresh token → Old refresh token deleted → Original API call retried with new JWT
flow_steps: API call made with expired JWT
Spring Boot checks refresh token in Redis
Valid: issue new JWT + new refresh token
Delete old refresh token from Redis
Retry original API call with new JWT
3.real_world: Industry standard pattern used by Razorpay, Stripe, all major fintech APIs

4.name: API Gateway & Rate Limiting
4.keywords: api gateway rate limit routing load balance security
4.protocols: HTTPS, SSL/TLS termination, REST, JWT validation
4.why_frontend: React talks to only one URL — the gateway. Gateway routes internally. Frontend never knows about individual microservices.
4.why_backend: Spring Cloud Gateway routes requests to correct microservice. Validates JWT before request reaches any service. Single entry point.
4.why_database: Redis counts requests per IP per minute for rate limiting. Counter increments per request. Expires after 60 seconds automatically.
4.why_pattern: All real fintech systems have an API Gateway. Single entry point for security, monitoring, rate limiting. Paytm gateway handles millions of requests per minute.
4.security: SSL/TLS termination at gateway, JWT validation before routing, Rate limit 100 requests per minute per IP, DDoS protection, IP blacklisting
4.rbi_rules: RBI mandates rate limiting to prevent abuse. All financial APIs must have access controls at entry point.
4.skills: Java, Spring Cloud Gateway, Redis, SSL certificates, NGINX
4.how_it_works: React sends request to gateway → SSL terminates → JWT validated → Rate limit checked in Redis → If within limit: route to correct microservice → If exceeded: return 429 Too Many Requests → Response returned to React
flow_steps: React sends HTTPS request to gateway
4.real_world: Razorpay, Paytm, PhonePe all use API Gateway as single entry point. Verified from engineering blogs.

5.name: Send Money (Basic Transfer)
5.keywords: send money transfer payment basic debit credit account
5.protocols: HTTPS, REST API, JWT, ACID Transactions, IMPS
5.why_frontend: React form validates amount and account before sending. Confirmation screen before final submit. Prevents accidental transfers.
5.why_backend: Spring Boot @Transactional annotation ensures atomicity. If credit fails after debit: automatic rollback. Money never lost.
5.why_database: PostgreSQL ACID guarantees. BEGIN TRANSACTION → Debit sender → Credit receiver → INSERT ledger → COMMIT. If any step fails: ROLLBACK both.
5.why_pattern: ACID transaction is non-negotiable for money movement. Either both accounts update or neither does. This is how every bank in the world works.
5.security: JWT mandatory, Transaction PIN, Idempotency key prevents duplicate payment, Full audit log, Amount limits per transaction
5.rbi_rules: RBI Payment System Guidelines, Transaction reference number mandatory, Debit before credit rule, Immediate confirmation to customer
5.skills: React, Java, Spring Boot, PostgreSQL, ACID transactions, JWT
5.how_it_works: User enters amount and recipient → React validates inputs → Spring Boot starts DB transaction → Checks sender balance sufficient → Debit sender account → Credit recipient account → Insert ledger record → Commit transaction → Send notification → Return success to React
flow_steps: UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - amount WHERE sender
UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + amount WHERE receiver
INSERT INTO ledger (txn_id, amount, sender, receiver, timestamp)
5.real_world: Core of every Indian bank IMPS transfer. Verified against NPCI IMPS specification.

6.name: UPI Payment
6.keywords: upi vpa unified payment interface bank transfer instant
6.protocols: HTTPS, UPI Protocol, IMPS, REST, Kafka, WebSocket
6.why_frontend: React validates UPI ID format (name@bank) before sending. WebSocket shows live payment status — Initiated, Processing, Success, Failed.
6.why_backend: Spring Boot validates UPI VPA, produces Kafka event. Bank Adapter translates to bank-specific API. Async because bank processing takes variable time.
6.why_database: MySQL for confirmed transactions. Redis for in-flight payment status — user checks live status via Redis key.
6.why_pattern: Event-Driven because UPI involves multiple banks. Each bank processes at different speeds. Kafka ensures no payment lost even if one bank is slow.
6.security: UPI PIN mandatory (never stored), Kafka message encryption, Device binding, Transaction limit Rs 100000
6.rbi_rules: NPCI UPI guidelines, Transaction limit Rs 100000 per transaction, Mandatory transaction reference, 24x7 availability required
6.skills: React, Java, Spring Boot, Apache Kafka, MySQL, Redis, WebSocket, NPCI UPI SDK
6.how_it_works: User enters UPI ID and amount → Spring Boot validates VPA exists → Produces Kafka payment event → Bank Adapter consumes → Calls payer bank API → Calls payee bank API → IMPS settlement → Kafka success event → Ledger updated → WebSocket pushes status to React
6.real_world: GPay, PhonePe, Paytm UPI flow. Verified against NPCI UPI technical specification document.

7.name: QR Code Payment
7.keywords: qr code scan payment generate merchant bharatqr
7.protocols: HTTPS, BharatQR Standard, UPI Deep Link, JWT
7.why_frontend: qrcode.react generates QR from UPI URL. react-qr-reader accesses camera for scanning. All browser-based, no native app needed.
7.why_backend: Spring Boot generates unique UPI payment URL encoded as QR. Validates scanned QR data. Checks QR not expired and not already used.
7.why_database: Redis stores QR with 10 minute TTL — QR auto-expires. Prevents QR reuse after payment completed.
7.why_pattern: Simple request-response for QR generation. One-time use QR ensures security.
7.security: QR expires in 10 minutes, One-time use enforced in Redis, Amount pre-set in QR prevents tampering
7.rbi_rules: NPCI BharatQR specification, QR must follow UPI deep link format, Merchant QR requires BBPS registration
7.skills: React, qrcode.react, react-qr-reader, Java, Spring Boot, Redis, MySQL
7.how_it_works: Merchant clicks Generate QR → Spring Boot creates UPI URL with amount and VPA → Encodes as QR → Stores QR ID in Redis with 10 min TTL → Customer scans → React decodes QR → Extracts UPI URL → Initiates UPI payment → On success: Redis marks QR used → Cannot be scanned again
7.real_world: PhonePe QR, Paytm QR, GPay QR all follow BharatQR + UPI deep link standard. Verified from NPCI BharatQR specification.

8.name: Payment with Fraud Detection
8.keywords: fraud detection suspicious transaction risk ml security block
8.protocols: HTTPS, REST API, JWT, Python-Java REST Bridge
8.why_frontend: React shows fraud check animation. Red warning overlay if fraud detected. User must explicitly confirm risky transaction.
8.why_backend: Spring Boot calls Python FastAPI fraud endpoint asynchronously. Payment processing continues while fraud check runs in parallel.
8.why_database: Redis caches fraud score per user for 1 hour — same user not checked twice unnecessarily. MySQL logs all fraud decisions for RBI audit.
8.why_pattern: ML Inference Gate — fraud model is mandatory checkpoint before ledger write. Verified pattern in Razorpay, Paytm fraud systems.
8.security: Random Forest checks: amount vs user average, time of day, location, velocity (txns per hour), device match, merchant category
8.rbi_rules: RBI fraud monitoring mandatory, Suspicious Transaction Report to FIU-India, Fraud above Rs 1 lakh reported within 2 hours
8.skills: Java, Spring Boot, Python, FastAPI, scikit-learn, Random Forest, Redis, MySQL
8.how_it_works: Transaction initiated → Spring Boot extracts 15 features → POST to FastAPI /predict/fraud → Random Forest model loaded from fraud_model.pkl → Predicts probability 0 to 1 → Above 0.7: BLOCK → 0.4 to 0.7: WARN user → Below 0.4: SAFE proceed → Decision logged in MySQL → Redis caches score
8.real_world: Razorpay fraud engine, Paytm risk system, PhonePe fraud ML. All use similar ML inference gate pattern.

9.name: Wallet Balance & Top Up
9.keywords: wallet balance topup load money add funds recharge
9.protocols: HTTPS, JWT, Razorpay Webhook, Cache-Aside Pattern
9.why_frontend: React shows balance from Redis cache instantly — no database query on every page load. Balance updates in real time via WebSocket.
9.why_backend: Spring Boot serves balance from Redis cache. On top-up: Razorpay webhook confirms payment → PostgreSQL ledger updated → Redis cache updated.
9.why_database: Redis for instant balance read (sub-millisecond). PostgreSQL for permanent ledger (ACID). CQRS: write to PostgreSQL, read from Redis.
9.why_pattern: CQRS: balance read 10000x more than written. Cache serves reads. Database serves writes. This is exactly how Paytm wallet works.
9.security: JWT, Razorpay webhook signature verification, Idempotency prevents double credit, Balance never goes negative enforced at DB level
9.rbi_rules: RBI PPI Master Directions 2021, Max wallet limit Rs 200000, Monthly top-up limit without full KYC Rs 10000, Loading only from linked bank account
9.skills: React, Java, Spring Boot, Redis, PostgreSQL, Razorpay API, WebSocket
9.how_it_works: User clicks Top Up → React shows Razorpay payment modal → User pays via card or UPI → Razorpay sends webhook to Spring Boot → Spring Boot verifies webhook signature → Begins PostgreSQL transaction → Inserts to ledger → Updates wallet balance → Commits → Updates Redis cache → WebSocket pushes new balance to React
flow_steps: Razorpay sends webhook to Spring Boot
Update Redis cache with new balance
9.real_world: Paytm Wallet, PhonePe Wallet top-up flow. Verified from Paytm engineering blog on CQRS.

10.name: Immutable Transaction Ledger
10.keywords: ledger immutable transaction record audit trail history permanent
10.why_frontend: React table shows transaction history with filters — date range, type, amount. Pagination for large history.
10.why_backend: Spring Boot only allows INSERT to ledger — no UPDATE, no DELETE. Immutability enforced at service layer.
10.why_database: PostgreSQL Write-Ahead Logging ensures every transaction is permanently recorded before confirming. Even server crash cannot lose a committed transaction.
10.why_pattern: Append-Only means transactions can never be edited or deleted. Regulators can audit complete history. Required by RBI.
10.security: No UPDATE or DELETE SQL allowed on ledger table, DB user has INSERT and SELECT only, All reads require JWT, Tamper detection via hash chaining
10.rbi_rules: RBI requires transaction records kept for minimum 10 years. Audit trail mandatory. No deletion of financial records allowed.
10.skills: Java, Spring Boot, PostgreSQL, SQL, ACID, WAL
10.how_it_works: Every transaction writes one row to ledger — never updates. Balance calculated by summing all ledger entries. If discrepancy found between Redis cache and ledger sum: ledger wins — it is always the truth.
flow_steps: INSERT new row with: txn_id, amount, type, timestamp, hash
Row hash = SHA256 of (previous hash + this transaction)
10.real_world: Jupiter Bank, RazorpayX use append-only ledger. Banking regulation requires this globally.

11.name: Balance Cache (Redis)
11.keywords: cache redis balance fast read performance speed
11.why_frontend: React displays balance instantly — loaded from Redis cache in under 1ms. No spinner needed.
11.why_backend: Spring Boot checks Redis first. If cache miss: reads PostgreSQL and populates Redis. Subsequent reads all from Redis.
11.why_database: Redis in-memory database. Balance read speed: Redis 0.1ms vs PostgreSQL 50ms. 500x faster for most common operation.
11.why_pattern: Cache-Aside: application manages cache manually. On write: update DB first then cache. On read: check cache first then DB. Prevents stale balance.
11.security: Cache invalidated immediately on any transaction. Balance in cache never shown if stale. Cache and DB always consistent.
11.rbi_rules: No specific rule but balance accuracy is legally mandatory. Cache must be invalidated within milliseconds of transaction.
11.skills: Java, Spring Boot, Redis, Jedis or Lettuce client
11.how_it_works: User opens wallet → React requests balance → Spring Boot checks Redis key wallet:userId:balance → Found: return immediately → Not found: query PostgreSQL → Store in Redis with 5 minute TTL → Return to React. On any transaction: invalidate Redis key immediately.
flow_steps: Spring Boot checks Redis: GET wallet:userId
On transaction: DEL wallet:userId from Redis
11.real_world: PhonePe wallet balance uses Redis cache. Verified from PhonePe tech blog on performance.

12.name: Loan Application Form
12.keywords: loan apply application personal home vehicle education borrow
12.protocols: HTTPS, JWT, REST API, Multipart Upload S3
12.why_frontend: React multi-step wizard: Personal Details → Financial Info → Documents → Review → Submit. Progress bar shows completion. Each step validates before allowing next.
12.why_backend: Spring Boot saves draft at each step — user can resume later. Final submit triggers pipeline. Handles multipart document upload to S3.
12.why_database: PostgreSQL stores application with all fields. AWS S3 stores uploaded documents securely with AES-256 encryption.
12.why_pattern: Multi-step form reduces abandonment — user not overwhelmed by one long form. Each step saves progress. Industry standard for loan applications.
12.security: JWT required, Document encryption S3, PII fields encrypted in PostgreSQL, Incomplete applications auto-deleted after 30 days
12.rbi_rules: RBI Fair Practices Code — all required information collected upfront. KYC mandatory before processing.
12.skills: React, Java, Spring Boot, PostgreSQL, AWS S3, multipart upload
12.how_it_works: User starts application → React step 1: personal details → Auto-saved to PostgreSQL as DRAFT → Step 2: financial info → Auto-saved → Step 3: document upload → Files sent to S3 → URLs stored in PostgreSQL → Step 4: review all details → Submit triggers credit scoring pipeline
12.real_world: CRED, MoneyTap, BajajFinserv loan application flow. Verified against KreditBee UX patterns.

13.name: EMI Calculator
13.keywords: emi calculator loan repayment monthly installment interest tenure
13.why_frontend: React calculates EMI in real time as user types — no server call needed for basic calculation. Chart.js donut chart shows principal vs interest split. Line chart shows outstanding balance over tenure.
13.why_backend: Spring Boot generates complete amortization schedule for entire tenure. Returns month-by-month breakdown of principal and interest.
13.why_database: PostgreSQL stores saved calculations. User can retrieve previous EMI calculations.
13.why_pattern: Compute-and-Display: calculation is pure math, no state needed. Frontend calculates instantly. Server only needed for saving schedule.
13.security: Input validation: loan amount 1000 to 10000000, tenure 1 to 360 months, rate 1 to 36 percent
13.rbi_rules: APR (Annual Percentage Rate) must be shown, not just interest rate. Processing fee must be included in true cost calculation.
13.skills: React, Chart.js, Java, Spring Boot, PostgreSQL, EMI formula: E = P * r * (1+r)^n / ((1+r)^n - 1)
13.how_it_works: User enters loan amount, rate, tenure → React instantly calculates EMI using formula → Chart.js renders breakdown → User saves → Spring Boot generates full schedule → PostgreSQL stores → User can download PDF
flow_steps: React calculates EMI instantly: E = P*r*(1+r)^n / ((1+r)^n - 1)
Chart.js renders principal vs interest donut
Chart.js renders outstanding balance line chart
13.real_world: BankBazaar, HDFC, ICICI EMI calculators. Formula verified against RBI EMI calculation guidelines.

14.name: Credit Scoring (ML)
14.keywords: credit score risk assessment ml loan approval xgboost predict
14.protocols: HTTPS, REST API, JWT, Python-Java REST Bridge
14.why_frontend: React gauge chart shows credit score 0 to 850. Color coded: red below 500, yellow 500-650, green above 650. Explains what score means.
14.why_backend: Spring Boot rule engine rejects obvious cases first (age, income minimums). FastAPI serves XGBoost model for borderline cases. Two stage pipeline saves ML compute.
14.why_database: Redis caches credit score for 24 hours — same applicant not scored twice in one day. PostgreSQL stores all scores with full audit trail.
14.why_pattern: Inference Pipeline: Rule Engine filters 40 percent applications cheaply. XGBoost only runs on remaining 60 percent. Verified in KreditBee CTO interview.
14.security: Score audit trail mandatory, Reason for rejection must be stored, PII features encrypted in feature store
14.rbi_rules: RBI Fair Practices Code: applicant must be told reason for rejection. Credit bureau (CIBIL/Experian) check mandatory before approval.
14.skills: Java, Spring Boot, Python, FastAPI, XGBoost, scikit-learn, PostgreSQL, Redis, CIBIL API
14.how_it_works: Application received → Rule engine: age 21+, income 15000+, employment verified → Pass: extract 15 features (debt-to-income, payment history, bureau score, loan-to-income, employment stability) → Feature store prepares vectors → XGBoost predicts default probability → Convert to score 0-850 → Above 700: Approve → 500-700: Manual review → Below 500: Reject → Store decision with reasons
flow_steps: Rule engine: check age, income, employment
14.real_world: CRED, KreditBee, MoneyTap credit scoring. Verified from KreditBee CTO conference talk 2023.

15.name: KYC Document Verification
15.keywords: kyc aadhaar pan document verification identity digilocker
15.protocols: HTTPS, JWT, DigiLocker API, Aadhaar OTP API, AWS S3 Pre-signed URL
15.why_frontend: React file upload with drag and drop. Shows upload progress. Preview of uploaded document. Validates file type (PDF, JPG) and size before upload.
15.why_backend: Spring Boot calls DigiLocker API to verify Aadhaar digitally. Extracts name, DOB, address automatically. Matches with application data.
15.why_database: S3 stores encrypted documents permanently — RBI requires 10 year retention. PostgreSQL stores verification status and extracted data.
15.why_pattern: Document Pipeline: upload → virus scan → format check → DigiLocker verify → data extraction → match check → approve or flag. Each stage is checkpointed.
15.security: AES-256 S3 encryption, Pre-signed URLs expire in 1 hour, Aadhaar number masked in all logs, Document watermarking
15.rbi_rules: KYC Master Directions 2016, DigiLocker accepted as valid KYC per RBI, Video KYC allowed for accounts up to Rs 1 lakh, Aadhaar based eKYC requires UIDAI license
15.skills: React, Java, Spring Boot, AWS S3, DigiLocker API, PostgreSQL
15.how_it_works: User uploads Aadhaar and PAN → React sends to Spring Boot → Spring Boot uploads to S3 → Calls DigiLocker API with Aadhaar number → User approves DigiLocker consent → DigiLocker returns verified name, DOB, address → Spring Boot matches with application → Match: KYC approved → Mismatch: flag for manual review
flow_steps: User uploads Aadhaar and PAN photos
React validates file type and size
Spring Boot uploads to AWS S3
Spring Boot matches with application data
15.real_world: BajajFinserv, CRED, Slice all use DigiLocker for KYC. Verified from RBI KYC circular and DigiLocker partner documentation.

16.name: Bill Payment (BBPS)
16.keywords: bill payment electricity water gas mobile broadband bbps
16.protocols: HTTPS, JWT, BBPS API, REST, IMPS for settlement
16.why_frontend: React fetches bill amount automatically from biller using consumer number. User just confirms and pays — no manual amount entry.
16.why_backend: Spring Boot BBPS Adapter fetches bill from BBPS network. BBPS connects to all billers in India — electricity, water, gas, telecom.
16.why_database: MySQL stores payment history per user. Simple queries for bill history sufficient — no complex analytics needed.
16.why_pattern: Synchronous request-response for bill fetch. User wants to see exact amount before paying. Then async payment confirmation.
16.security: JWT mandatory, Consumer number validation, Payment confirmation before debit, Duplicate payment prevention via idempotency
16.rbi_rules: BBPS operated by NPCI, All bill payments above Rs 50000 require additional authentication, Receipt mandatory for every payment
16.skills: React, Java, Spring Boot, MySQL, BBPS API, NPCI integration
16.how_it_works: User enters consumer number and selects biller → Spring Boot calls BBPS API → BBPS fetches bill from biller → Returns bill amount and due date → User confirms → Spring Boot processes payment via IMPS → BBPS settles with biller → MySQL records payment → Notification sent → Digital receipt generated
flow_steps: Spring Boot calls BBPS fetch API
BBPS returns bill amount and due date
React shows bill details to user
Spring Boot initiates IMPS payment to BBPS
MySQL records payment with receipt number
Notification sent via SMS and app
16.real_world: GPay Bills, PhonePe Bills, Paytm Bills all use BBPS. Verified from NPCI BBPS technical documentation.

17.name: Auto Pay & Recurring (NACH)
17.keywords: autopay recurring nach mandate subscription automatic debit
17.protocols: HTTPS, JWT, NACH API NPCI, Cron Expression, Spring Scheduler
17.why_frontend: React toggle enables auto pay per subscription. Shows next debit date and amount clearly. Can cancel anytime.
17.why_backend: Spring @Scheduled runs at midnight. Queries MySQL for mandates due today. Processes in batch. Handles failures with retry.
17.why_database: MySQL stores NACH mandate details. Redis caches active mandates for fast batch processing at midnight.
17.why_pattern: Batch at midnight processes all due mandates together. More efficient than processing individually throughout day. Matches NPCI NACH batch settlement window.
17.security: Bank mandate verification, Pre-debit notification 3 days mandatory, Maximum debit amount set at mandate creation, Cancellation immediately effective
17.rbi_rules: RBI NACH framework, Pre-debit notification mandatory 3 days before, Mandate amendment requires fresh bank authorization, Maximum debit amount mandatory field
17.skills: Java, Spring Boot, Spring Scheduler, MySQL, Redis, NACH NPCI API
17.how_it_works: User enables auto pay → Spring Boot registers NACH mandate with bank → MySQL stores mandate → Redis caches active mandates → Cron runs midnight → Fetches mandates due today from Redis → Calls NACH API for each → Bank debits account → Success: MySQL updated, notification sent → Failure: retry 3 times → All failed: alert user
flow_steps: User enables auto pay for subscription
Spring Boot registers NACH mandate with bank
MySQL stores: userId, amount, frequency, startDate
Call NACH API for each mandate
17.real_world: Amazon Prime, Netflix India, Spotify use NACH for recurring billing. Verified from NPCI NACH operational guidelines.

18.name: Bill Reminder & Notification
18.keywords: reminder notification alert due date bill push sms email
18.protocols: HTTPS, FCM, SMTP, Twilio SMS, WebSocket, Cron
18.why_frontend: React subscribes to FCM for push notifications. Toast component shows in-app alerts. Notification bell with unread count.
18.why_backend: Spring @Scheduled checks bills due in next 3 days. Sends FCM push + SMS + email. @Async ensures notifications do not block main thread.
18.why_database: MySQL notification_log records every notification sent. Prevents duplicate notifications. Tracks delivery status.
18.why_pattern: Scheduled Push: notifications are time-triggered not event-triggered. Cron is the right pattern for deadline-based alerts.
18.security: TRAI DLT registration mandatory for SMS, FCM token refreshed on each app open, Unsubscribe not allowed for payment alerts per RBI
18.rbi_rules: RBI mandates transaction alerts within 30 seconds. Bill payment reminders best practice. NACH pre-debit notification mandatory 3 days before.
18.skills: Java, Spring Boot, Firebase FCM, Twilio, JavaMail, Spring Scheduler, MySQL
18.how_it_works: Cron runs 9am daily → Queries MySQL for bills due in next 3 days → For each: send FCM push notification → Send SMS via Twilio → Send email via JavaMail → Log in notification_log → User sees notification on phone and in app
flow_steps: SELECT bills WHERE due_date BETWEEN today AND today+3
User sees toast in React app
18.real_world: Google Pay, PhonePe bill reminders. RBI mandates this for NACH per circular.

19.name: Spending Categorizer (ML/NLP)
19.keywords: spending category nlp classify food travel shopping expense tag
19.protocols: HTTPS, JWT, REST API, Python-Java Bridge
19.why_frontend: React pie chart shows spending by category. Each slice clickable — shows transactions in that category. Monthly view and weekly view toggle.
19.why_backend: Spring Boot sends merchant name to Python FastAPI. Naive Bayes model classifies into category. Result stored with transaction.
19.why_database: MySQL stores transactions with category tag. Aggregate queries for monthly spending per category are simple SQL GROUP BY.
19.why_pattern: NLP Classification: merchant name is text. Naive Bayes is perfect for text classification — fast, accurate for this use case. Verified in Groww transaction categorization.
19.security: Transaction data private per user, Category stored locally not sent to third parties
19.rbi_rules: Spending data is sensitive PII. Must be encrypted at rest. User consent required to share with third parties.
19.skills: React, Chart.js, Java, Spring Boot, Python, FastAPI, Naive Bayes, scikit-learn, MySQL
19.how_it_works: Transaction recorded → Merchant name extracted (eg: ZOMATO, UBER, AMAZON) → POST to FastAPI /predict/category → Naive Bayes classifies → Returns: Food, Transport, Shopping, Entertainment, Utilities, Healthcare → MySQL stores category with transaction → React Chart.js shows spending breakdown
flow_steps: React Chart.js updates spending pie chart
19.real_world: Groww, CRED, Jupiter all use NLP transaction categorization. Verified from Groww product feature description.

20.name: Portfolio Tracker
20.keywords: portfolio stocks mutual fund investment holdings returns pnl
20.protocols: HTTPS, JWT, NSE/BSE API, WebSocket, REST
20.why_frontend: React table shows each holding with live P&L calculated on frontend using cached prices. Chart.js line chart shows portfolio value over time.
20.why_backend: Spring Boot fetches live prices from NSE API and caches in Redis. Portfolio P&L calculated server-side using holding quantity and current price.
20.why_database: PostgreSQL for holdings (structured relational data). MongoDB for historical prices (time-series document model). Redis for current live prices.
20.why_pattern: Read-Heavy Cache: prices read thousands of times per second. Redis serves all reads. NSE API only called once per price update.
20.security: JWT, Portfolio data private per user, Read-only NSE API key, HTTPS for all price feeds
20.rbi_rules: SEBI Investment Advisor Regulations for advisory features. Risk disclosure mandatory. KYC through KRA mandatory.
20.skills: React, Chart.js, Java, Spring Boot, PostgreSQL, MongoDB, Redis, NSE/BSE API, WebSocket
20.how_it_works: User adds holdings (stock, quantity, buy price) → PostgreSQL stores → Spring Boot fetches live price from NSE → Caches in Redis → Calculates P&L: (current price - buy price) x quantity → React shows table with P&L → Green for profit, red for loss → Risk Profiler ML suggests portfolio rebalancing
flow_steps: User adds holding: stock, quantity, buy price
WebSocket subscribes to NSE live prices
Spring Boot calculates P&L per holding
Risk Profiler ML runs weekly rebalancing suggestion
20.real_world: Zerodha Kite, Groww portfolio tracker. Verified from Zerodha engineering blog on Redis usage.

21.name: Live Price Feed
21.keywords: live price realtime websocket stock crypto ticker feed streaming
21.protocols: WebSocket WSS, Redis Pub/Sub, NSE/BSE Feed, Binance API for crypto
21.why_frontend: React WebSocket client receives price every second. Updates price display without any page refresh or API polling. Polling would cause 100x more server load.
21.why_backend: Spring Boot WebSocket server distributes prices to all connected clients via Redis Pub/Sub. One NSE connection serves thousands of users.
21.why_database: Redis stores current price — updated every second. MongoDB stores OHLCV history for charts — document model perfect for time-series.
21.why_pattern: WebSocket Streaming: prices change every second. HTTP polling every second = millions of unnecessary requests. WebSocket = one persistent connection, server pushes updates.
21.security: WSS (WebSocket Secure) mandatory, JWT authentication on WebSocket handshake, Rate limit per connection
21.rbi_rules: SEBI requires real-time data to be sourced from authorized exchanges only (NSE, BSE). No fabricated prices.
21.skills: React, WebSocket API, Java, Spring Boot WebSocket, Redis Pub/Sub, MongoDB, NSE API
21.how_it_works: Spring Boot connects to NSE WebSocket feed → Receives price every second → Publishes to Redis Pub/Sub channel → All Spring Boot instances receive → Each pushes to connected React clients → React updates price display → MongoDB stores OHLCV every minute → Price Trend ML analyses pattern
flow_steps: Spring Boot connects to NSE WebSocket
All server instances receive via Pub/Sub
Each server pushes to connected clients
Price Trend ML runs every 5 minutes
21.real_world: Zerodha Kite live prices use WebSocket. Verified from Zerodha engineering blog and Kite API documentation.

22.name: Financial Charts & Reports
22.keywords: charts graphs visualization candlestick dashboard analytics reports d3
22.protocols: HTTPS, JWT, REST API, PDF generation
22.why_frontend: Chart.js for standard charts (pie, bar, line). D3.js for complex financial charts (candlestick, heatmap) — Chart.js cannot do these well.
22.why_backend: Python Pandas aggregates large transaction datasets efficiently. Java cannot match Pandas for data aggregation and statistical calculations.
22.why_database: PostgreSQL OLAP for pre-aggregated data — queries in seconds not minutes. MongoDB for raw time-series data before aggregation.
22.why_pattern: Data Visualization: pre-aggregate data on server, send only summary to frontend. Never send raw data to React — too large.
22.security: JWT, Data visible only to account owner, PDF report requires re-authentication
22.rbi_rules: Financial reports must be accurate. Historical data retention 10 years. Export in standard formats.
22.skills: React, Chart.js, D3.js, Java, Spring Boot, Python, Pandas, PostgreSQL, MongoDB
22.how_it_works: User opens dashboard → React requests chart data → Spring Boot calls Python Pandas service → Pandas aggregates transactions by category and month → Returns summary data → Chart.js renders pie chart for categories → D3.js renders candlestick for price history → User can export as PDF
flow_steps: React requests chart data for date range
Spring Boot calls Python Pandas service
Pandas aggregates: GROUP BY category, month
22.real_world: Zerodha Kite uses D3.js for candlestick charts. Groww uses Chart.js for portfolio charts. Verified from their open source repositories.

23.name: Digital Wallet & Immutable Ledger (Complete)
23.keywords: digital wallet complete full immutable ledger paytm phonepe
23.protocols: HTTPS, JWT, OAuth 2.0, BCrypt, Cache-Aside, ACID, WebSocket, Razorpay Webhook, FCM
23.why_frontend: React SPA with Redux for global wallet state. Protected routes. WebSocket for live balance. Chart.js for spending analytics.
23.why_backend: Separate microservices: Auth, Wallet, Ledger, Notification. CQRS: Redis serves all balance reads, PostgreSQL for all writes.
23.why_database: PostgreSQL Write-Ahead Logging ensures immutable ledger. Redis cache makes balance reads 500x faster than PostgreSQL.
23.why_pattern: CQRS verified in Paytm and PhonePe architecture. Balance is read 10000x per second. Cache serves reads. PostgreSQL serves writes only.
23.security: BCrypt, JWT, Redis blacklist, 2FA, Fraud ML every transaction, AES-256 encryption, Rate limiting, IP velocity
23.rbi_rules: RBI PPI Master Directions 2021, KYC above Rs 10000, Max wallet Rs 200000, India data localisation, FIU reporting
23.skills: React, Redux, WebSocket, Java, Spring Boot, Spring Security, PostgreSQL, Redis, Python, scikit-learn, Docker, Razorpay
23.how_it_works: Register → KYC → Wallet created → Top up via Razorpay → Webhook confirms → Ledger records → Redis updates → Send money → JWT verify → Fraud ML check → ACID transaction → Immutable ledger entry → Redis both balances updated → WebSocket notifies both users → SMS confirmation
flow_steps: Redis cache initialised with zero balance
Top up: Razorpay modal → payment → webhook
PostgreSQL ACID: INSERT ledger + UPDATE balance
SAFE: ACID debit sender + credit receiver
WebSocket push to both React apps
23.real_world: Paytm Wallet architecture. Verified from Paytm Engineering blog and PhonePe tech talks.

24.name: P2P Payment Gateway (Complete)
24.keywords: p2p payment gateway complete upi kafka event driven realtime
24.protocols: HTTPS, JWT, UPI Protocol, Apache Kafka, WebSocket, IMPS, BharatQR, FCM, Twilio
24.why_frontend: React QR generator and scanner. WebSocket shows live payment status to both sender and receiver simultaneously.
24.why_backend: Kafka decouples payment stages. Fraud Worker and Ledger Service are independent consumers — failure in one does not affect other.
24.why_database: MySQL for confirmed transactions. Redis for in-flight status — user sees Processing → Verified → Sent in real time.
24.why_pattern: Event-Driven verified in NPCI UPI whitepaper. Multiple banks involved means async processing mandatory.
24.security: UPI PIN never stored, Kafka message encryption, Idempotency keys, Fraud ML, Device binding, Transaction limits
24.rbi_rules: NPCI UPI guidelines, Rs 100000 per transaction, 24x7 availability, Mandatory transaction reference, Chargeback within 3 days
24.skills: React, qrcode.react, WebSocket, Java, Spring Boot, Kafka, MySQL, Redis, Python, NPCI UPI SDK
24.how_it_works: Enter UPI ID → Validate VPA → Produce Kafka event → Fraud Worker consumes → ML checks → SAFE → Ledger Consumer → ACID transaction → MySQL debit + credit → Redis status SUCCESS → WebSocket push to both users → SMS both
flow_steps: Sender enters receiver UPI ID and amount
Fraud Worker consumes: ML fraud check
ACID: debit sender + credit receiver in MySQL
WebSocket push SUCCESS to sender React
WebSocket push RECEIVED to receiver React
24.real_world: GPay, PhonePe P2P flow. Verified against NPCI UPI technical specification v2.0.

25.name: AI-Driven Lending & Credit Score (Complete)
25.keywords: lending complete ai credit loan nbfc full application scoring
25.ml_needed: Yes - XGBoost Credit Risk + Rule Engine
25.protocols: HTTPS, JWT, DigiLocker API, CIBIL API, NACH NPCI, SMTP, Twilio, S3 Pre-signed URL
25.why_frontend: Multi-step wizard reduces abandonment. Chart.js shows credit score gauge and EMI breakdown chart. Document upload with progress.
25.why_backend: Spring Boot pipeline orchestrates all stages. FastAPI serves XGBoost for ML scoring. Rule engine in Java for fast pre-filtering.
25.why_database: PostgreSQL for complex loan calculations. S3 for encrypted documents. Redis for credit score cache 24 hours.
25.why_pattern: Inference Pipeline verified in KreditBee CTO interview. Rule engine eliminates 40 percent obviously ineligible applications. ML runs on remaining 60 percent.
25.security: Document AES-256 encryption, PII masking in logs, RBAC for loan officers, Complete audit trail
25.rbi_rules: Fair Practices Code, NBFC registration, APR disclosure, 3 day cooling off, KYC Master Directions, CIBIL check mandatory
25.skills: React, Chart.js, Java, Spring Boot, Python, XGBoost, PostgreSQL, Redis, S3, DigiLocker, CIBIL, NACH
25.how_it_works: Apply → KYC DigiLocker → Rule engine filter → Feature Store prepares 15 features → XGBoost scores → Decision → EMI schedule → NACH mandate → Monthly Cron collects EMI → Full notification at every step
flow_steps: Fail: auto reject with specific reason
Pass: Feature Store prepares 15 ML features
Monthly Cron collects EMI on due date
SMS + email at every status change
25.real_world: KreditBee, CRED, MoneyTap lending pipeline. Verified from KreditBee CTO conference talk 2023.

26.name: Automated Bill & Subscription Manager (Complete)
26.keywords: bill manager complete subscription autopay scheduler automated full
26.protocols: HTTPS, JWT, BBPS API, NACH NPCI, FCM, Twilio, SMTP, Cron Expression, Spring Scheduler
26.why_frontend: React calendar view shows all bills with due dates. Chart.js bar chart shows monthly bill totals. Toggle auto pay per bill.
26.why_backend: Spring @Scheduled processes all due bills at midnight. Efficient batch over individual processing. BBPS adapter handles all Indian billers.
26.why_database: MySQL simple relational structure perfect for subscription management. No complex queries needed.
26.why_pattern: Batch Processing verified against BBPS architecture document. Nightly batch matches NPCI settlement windows.
26.security: NACH mandate bank verification, Pre-debit notification mandatory, PIN for manual payments, Failure alerts
26.rbi_rules: BBPS NPCI guidelines, NACH pre-debit mandatory, Dispute resolution 3 days, Receipt every payment
26.skills: React, Chart.js, Java, Spring Boot, Spring Scheduler, MySQL, BBPS API, NACH, FCM, Twilio, Python Naive Bayes
26.how_it_works: Add bills → MySQL stores → BBPS fetches amounts → Cron daily reminder D-3 → Due date midnight: batch processes all → BBPS/NACH payment → MySQL updated → Spending Classifier tags category → Charts updated → Confirmations sent
flow_steps: User adds bill: type, consumer number, auto pay toggle
D-3: Cron sends FCM + SMS reminder
Due date midnight: Cron runs batch
Fetch all due subscriptions from MySQL
For each: process via BBPS or NACH
26.real_world: Google Pay Bills, PhonePe Bills use BBPS. Verified from NPCI BBPS operational guidelines document.

27.name: Visual Financial Analytics Dashboard (Complete)
27.keywords: analytics dashboard complete portfolio stocks crypto charts lambda big data
27.ml_needed: Yes - Risk Profiler + NLP Categorizer + Price Trend Classifier
27.protocols: HTTPS, JWT, WebSocket, Kafka, NSE/BSE API, Binance API, REST, Pandas
27.why_frontend: Chart.js for pie and bar charts. D3.js for candlestick charts — Chart.js cannot do this. WebSocket for live prices every second.
27.why_backend: Spring Boot for REST and WebSocket. Python Pandas for heavy analytics — Sharpe ratio, correlation, returns calculation. Java cannot match Pandas here.
27.why_database: PostgreSQL OLAP for aggregated analytics data. MongoDB for OHLCV time-series — 10x faster than PostgreSQL for this. Redis for live prices.
27.why_pattern: Lambda Architecture verified in Zerodha Kite engineering blog. Batch for historical analysis. Speed Layer for live market hours prices. Serving Layer merges both.
27.security: JWT, Portfolio private per user, Read-only exchange API keys, SEBI audit trail
27.rbi_rules: SEBI Investment Advisor Regulations 2013, Risk disclosure mandatory, SEBI registered intermediary for advisory, KYC via KRA
27.skills: React, Chart.js, D3.js, WebSocket, Java, Spring Boot, Python, Pandas, NumPy, PostgreSQL, MongoDB, Redis, Kafka, NSE API, Binance API
27.how_it_works: Historical prices processed nightly by Batch Layer via Pandas → Stored in PostgreSQL OLAP → Market hours: NSE WebSocket feeds Speed Layer → Redis updated every second → React WebSocket receives live price → D3.js updates candlestick chart → Risk Profiler ML checks portfolio allocation weekly → NLP tags all transactions → Price Trend ML predicts direction
flow_steps: Aggregated data stored in PostgreSQL OLAP
Live prices flow into Speed Layer
Redis updated every second per symbol
All transactions tagged by NLP Categorizer
Price Trend ML predicts next day direction
User sees BULLISH or BEARISH signal
27.real_world: Zerodha Kite, Groww, Smallcase. Zerodha explicitly uses Lambda Architecture. Verified from Zerodha engineering blog.

28.name: Wallet + Fraud Detection
28.keywords: wallet fraud security risk detection combined
28.protocols: HTTPS, JWT, Cache-Aside, ACID, REST Bridge, WebSocket
28.why_frontend: Fraud status shown live. Warning overlay for suspicious transactions.
28.why_backend: Fraud check mandatory gate before any wallet transaction writes to ledger.
28.why_database: PostgreSQL immutable ledger. Redis balance cache. MySQL fraud decision log.
28.why_pattern: CQRS for wallet reads. ML Inference Gate for fraud before writes.
28.security: ML fraud model, JWT, 2FA, Velocity checks, AES-256 encryption
28.rbi_rules: RBI PPI + Fraud monitoring circular. FIU reporting mandatory.
28.skills: React, Java, Spring Boot, Python, scikit-learn, PostgreSQL, Redis
28.how_it_works: Every wallet transaction passes through Fraud Engine before ledger write. Real time fraud status shown in React.
flow_steps: Transaction → JWT verify → Fraud ML check → SAFE → ACID ledger write → Redis cache update → WebSocket notify

29.name: Wallet + Analytics
29.why_frontend: Wallet balance + spending charts on same dashboard.
29.why_backend: Every transaction auto-tagged by NLP Categorizer. Pandas aggregates for charts.
29.why_database: PostgreSQL for ledger and aggregated analytics. Redis for balance cache.
29.why_pattern: CQRS for wallet. Lambda for analytics aggregation.
29.skills: React, Chart.js, Java, Spring Boot, Python, Pandas, Naive Bayes, PostgreSQL, Redis
29.how_it_works: Transaction recorded → NLP tags category → Pandas aggregates monthly → Chart.js shows breakdown alongside wallet balance.
flow_steps: Transaction → Ledger → NLP categorize → Pandas aggregate → Chart.js render

30.why_pattern: Event-Driven for payments. Inference Pipeline for lending decisions.

31.name: Full Banking Suite
31.keywords: banking complete suite wallet payment analytics combined full

31.why_backend: Each feature as independent microservice. API Gateway routes. Shared Auth across all services.

31.why_pattern: Microservices: each feature deployed and scaled independently. Industry standard for full banking.

31.rbi_rules: All applicable RBI guidelines: PPI, NPCI UPI, BBPS, fraud monitoring

32.name: Complete Fintech Platform
32.keywords: complete fintech all platform everything combined full suite
32.backend: Spring Boot Microservices + Python FastAPI + Kafka
32.ml_needed: Yes - All 3 ML Models (Fraud + Credit + Spending)
